artwork_file: George Stubbs_c.1763_Zebra.jpg
artwork_description: **George Stubbs** painted this extraordinary study from life, depicting the zebra presented to Queen Charlotte as a gift and kept at *Buckingham House* in London. Executed around 1763 when Stubbs was establishing himself as England's foremost animal painter, the work demonstrates his scientific precision in rendering an exotic creature then rarely seen in Britain. The animal's distinctive striped coat is rendered with meticulous accuracy, the result of close observation rather than fanciful embellishment. Now in the *Yale Center for British Art*, the painting stands as one of the earliest and finest depictions of a zebra in Western art. ***It confirms Stubbs's unique ability to unite empirical exactitude with the dignity of monumental animal portraiture.***

artwork_file: George Stubbs_c.1765_Newmarket Heath, with a Rubbing-down House.jpg
artwork_title: Newmarket Heath, with a Rubbing-down House
artwork_description: **George Stubbs** painted this panoramic view of *Newmarket Heath*, the heartland of English horse racing, around 1765, showing the rubbing-down house near the finish of the famous Beacon Course. Stable hands and jockeys attend to horses against a vast, luminous sky, the flat Suffolk landscape evoking the spacious freedom of the turf. Stubbs was a regular presence at *Newmarket* throughout the 1760s, retained by aristocratic patrons including the Marquess of Rockingham to record their prize racehorses. The composition balances the grandeur of the open heath with the intimate world of the stable and the men who worked within it. ***Now at *Tate Britain*, it is one of the defining images of 18th-century sporting life in England.***

artwork_file: George Stubbs_1780-1784_Phaeton with a Pair of Cream Ponies and a Stable-Lad.jpg
artwork_title: Phaeton with a Pair of Cream Ponies and a Stable-Lad
artwork_description: **George Stubbs** executed this elegant work in beeswax and oil on panel between 1780 and 1784, demonstrating his mastery of an unusual medium he pioneered in his later career. A light two-wheeled phaeton drawn by a handsome pair of cream ponies is attended by a stable-lad in livery, the composition celebrating the fashionable pastime of carriage driving among the late 18th-century aristocracy. The pale warm tones of the ponies' coats are rendered with extraordinary delicacy, the beeswax medium lending the surface a distinctive luminosity. Stubbs brought the same anatomical precision to carriage horses that he applied to racehorses, treating every subject with equal seriousness. ***Now in the *Yale Center for British Art*, the painting is a refined document of Georgian leisure and wealth.***

artwork_file: George Stubbs_c.1768_Mares and Foals in a River Landscape.jpg
artwork_title: Mares and Foals in a River Landscape
artwork_description: **George Stubbs** was drawn repeatedly to the subject of mares and foals throughout the 1760s, producing a series of pastoral compositions that rank among his finest achievements. This example, painted around 1768, shows a group of thoroughbred mares and their foals arranged in a gently undulating river landscape, the horses placed in studied formation against a calm sky. The work reflects the keen interest of Stubbs's aristocratic patrons in breeding and bloodstock, as the careful recording of each animal's individual markings and conformation suggests. Stubbs modelled each horse from life, and his anatomical knowledge - gained from 18 months dissecting horses at *Horkstow, Lincolnshire* - ensured an accuracy no rival painter could match. ***Now at *Tate Britain*, it is a masterpiece of equine portraiture that transcends mere sporting record.***

artwork_file: George Stubbs_1793_Soldiers of the 10th Light Dragoons.jpg
artwork_description: **George Stubbs** painted this imposing group portrait in 1793 for the Prince of Wales, recording troopers of the *10th Light Dragoons* - the regiment of which the future George IV was colonel - in full review order. Three mounted soldiers and their elegant horses are arranged with the formal precision of a parade, their splendid uniforms and accoutrements rendered with painstaking accuracy. The painting is a companion work to *Laetitia, Lady Lade* of the same year, both commissioned as part of the same royal patronage. Stubbs was then in his late sixties yet painted with undiminished power, the horses displaying all of his characteristic anatomical command. ***Now in the *Royal Collection*, it is among the finest British military sporting paintings of the 18th century.***

artwork_file: George Stubbs_c.1760_The Countess of Coningsby in the Costume of the Charlton Hunt.jpg
artwork_title: The Countess of Coningsby in the Costume of the Charlton Hunt
artwork_description: **George Stubbs** painted this rare equestrian portrait of a woman around 1760, shortly after his arrival in London from his years of anatomical study at *Horkstow*. The Countess of Coningsby is depicted side-saddle in the distinctive uniform of the celebrated *Charlton Hunt* in Sussex, one of the most fashionable hunts of the period. The composition is strikingly assured for an artist still building his reputation, the horse and rider placed against a simple neutral landscape that focuses all attention on the subject. Stubbs's ability to individualize both horse and human sitter is already fully evident, foreshadowing the masterworks of the following decade. ***Now at the *Yale Center for British Art*, it is one of the earliest and most distinguished female equestrian portraits in the English tradition.***

artwork_file: George Stubbs_1780_Greenland Falcon.jpg
artwork_description: **George Stubbs** executed this striking ornithological study in beeswax on panel in 1780, turning his analytical eye from horses to one of the most powerful birds of prey in the world. The Greenland Falcon - one of the rarest and most prized falconry birds of the era - is rendered with scientific precision, its pale plumage, sharp talons, and fierce gaze commanding the entire painted surface. Stubbs had access to live exotic animals through the private menageries of his aristocratic patrons, and he painted each creature with the same rigour he brought to his equine work. The choice of beeswax medium, which Stubbs explored extensively in this period, gives the surface an unusual warmth and translucency. ***Now at the *Yale Center for British Art*, the work demonstrates that Stubbs was as gifted a natural-history painter as he was an animal portraitist.***

artwork_file: George Stubbs_1770_Horse Frightened by a Lion.jpg
artwork_description: **George Stubbs** explored the theme of a horse terrified by a lurking predator over a period of approximately thirty years, producing some seventeen paintings on the subject. This 1770 canvas focuses on an early episode of the drama: a white horse recoiling in terror from the presence of a lion not yet fully engaged. The horse's arched neck, flared nostrils, and wide eyes convey panic with extraordinary psychological intensity, the result of Stubbs's deep knowledge of equine anatomy and behaviour. The subject, inspired by an antique Roman marble sculpture Stubbs had seen at the *Palazzo dei Conservatori* in Rome in 1754, became one of the defining themes of early Romanticism. ***This painting anticipates by decades the emotional violence that would characterise the Romantic movement in France and England.***

artwork_file: George Stubbs_1763-1765_Mares and Foals on a River Bank.jpg
artwork_title: Mares and Foals on a River Bank
artwork_description: **George Stubbs** painted this harmonious composition between 1763 and 1765, during the most productive decade of his career when aristocratic commissions were arriving in abundance. A group of thoroughbred mares and foals are arranged along a riverside bank, their rhythmic poses creating a frieze-like composition of rare elegance. The subtle gradations of the horses' coats - bay, chestnut, grey - demonstrate Stubbs's unrivalled capacity for rendering equine colour and texture in oil. The pastoral setting, with its softly lit sky and lush vegetation, places the horses in an idealized English landscape that flatters both the animals and their owners. ***The work is among the finest of Stubbs's celebrated *Mares and Foals* series, which collectively represent the pinnacle of 18th-century equine painting in Britain.***

artwork_file: George Stubbs_1765_Gimcrack on Newmarket Heath.jpg
artwork_description: **George Stubbs** immortalized the famous grey racehorse *Gimcrack* in this celebrated 1765 painting, capturing one of the most brilliant thoroughbreds of the era at *Newmarket Heath*. Gimcrack, a diminutive but extraordinarily fast grey, won 26 of his 36 races and became one of the legendary figures of the English turf; the annual *Gimcrack Stakes* at York still bears his name. Stubbs shows the horse with a trainer, a stable-lad, and a jockey in the vast open landscape of the Heath, combining intimate stable detail with grand sporting panorama. The painting sold at *Christie's* in July 2011 for GBP22.4 million, setting the auction record for Stubbs. ***It remains the defining image of *Gimcrack* and one of the supreme examples of the English sporting painting tradition.***

artwork_file: George Stubbs_1768_Two Gentlemen Going a Shooting.jpg
artwork_description: **George Stubbs** depicted two sportsmen setting out with their dogs against a landscape featuring *Creswell Crags*, the dramatic limestone gorge on the Nottinghamshire-Derbyshire border that he returned to several times as a backdrop. The two gentlemen, rendered as confident individual portraits, carry their guns across a sunlit upland landscape while their dogs range ahead, the composition capturing the easy pleasures of rural sport among the Georgian gentry. Stubbs was unique among sporting painters in his ability to characterise the human figures in his shooting scenes with the same attentive individuality he brought to the animals. The painting belongs to a group of related shooting scenes from the late 1760s, now largely distributed between *Yale* and *Warsaw*. ***It is an authoritative record of aristocratic sport painted with the precision of a natural historian and the eye of a portraitist.***

artwork_file: George Stubbs_c.1780_White Poodle in a Punt.jpg
artwork_description: **George Stubbs** painted this intimate and enchanting portrait of a white poodle poised in a wooden punt around 1780, one of many single-dog portraits he undertook from the 1770s onwards at the request of wealthy patrons. The poodle's thick, pale coat is rendered with extraordinary tactile delicacy, its alert pose and direct gaze endowing the animal with a personality as vivid as any human sitter. Stubbs brought to all his dog paintings the same anatomical rigour that informed his equine work, having studied canine as well as equine anatomy in depth. The punt and the soft landscape beyond place the dog in a specifically English waterside setting, adding a note of leisure and domesticity. ***Now in the *National Gallery of Art* in Washington, it is one of the most refined and affecting animal portraits of the 18th century.***

artwork_file: George Stubbs_1791_A Bay Horse with a Groom.jpg
artwork_title: A Bay Horse with a Groom
artwork_description: **George Stubbs** painted this accomplished equestrian portrait in 1791, when he was enjoying the patronage of the Prince of Wales and working on an extended series of royal horse subjects. A well-bred bay horse is shown with a groom in livery, the pair presented against an unelaborated landscape that focuses attention entirely on the beauty of the animal. Stubbs always painted grooms as individuals, giving each attendant a distinct physiognomy that prevents them from becoming mere accessories to their equine charges. The painting belongs to a group of horse-and-groom subjects of the early 1790s, all executed for the Royal Collection. ***Now in the *Royal Collection*, it exemplifies Stubbs's late style: confidently economical and anatomically impeccable.***

artwork_file: George Stubbs_1793_Laetitia, Lady Lade.jpg
artwork_description: **George Stubbs** painted this commanding equestrian portrait in 1793 as a companion piece to *Soldiers of the 10th Light Dragoons*, both works executed for the Prince of Wales. Lady Lade, one of the finest horsewomen of her generation, is shown mounted on a spirited grey horse and riding side-saddle with effortless authority, her confident bearing a testament to her celebrated skill. Laetitia Lade was a colourful and controversial figure in Regency society, famous for her fearless riding and her close friendship with the Prince of Wales. Stubbs renders both horse and rider with equal conviction, his command of equine anatomy at the age of nearly seventy as assured as ever. ***Now in the *Royal Collection*, the portrait is among the great equestrian images of 18th-century British art.***

artwork_file: George Stubbs_1770_A Repose after Shooting.jpg
artwork_description: **George Stubbs** painted this relaxed sporting scene in 1770, showing two gentlemen resting from the day's shoot alongside their dogs and equipment in a shaded outdoor setting. The composition balances the informal ease of the figures with Stubbs's characteristic precision in the rendering of the dogs, game bags, and guns that surround them. The painting belongs to a group of related shooting sequences produced around 1768-1770, in which Stubbs documented the complete narrative arc of a day's sport, from setting out to the journey home. Unlike traditional hunting and shooting pictures, Stubbs's scenes treat both humans and dogs as individuals rather than as conventional sporting types. ***Now at the *Yale Center for British Art*, it is a companionable and warmly observed record of Georgian countryside life.***

artwork_file: George Stubbs_c.1760_Foxhound.jpg
artwork_medium: Oil on paper laid on board
artwork_description: **George Stubbs** painted this close study of a foxhound around 1760, in the medium of oil on paper laid on board that he favoured for certain smaller-scale animal studies. A single hound is shown in profile against a plain background, the composition stripped of all landscape distraction to focus entirely on the dog's anatomy and bearing. Stubbs brought an uncompromising scientific rigour to his paintings of hounds, treating each animal as a subject worthy of the same sustained analysis he gave his celebrated horses. The work belongs to a group of closely related foxhound studies from the early 1760s, a period in which Stubbs was establishing his reputation as the foremost animal painter in England. ***The painting is a masterclass in disciplined observation, combining naturalistic accuracy with an austere formal beauty.***

artwork_file: George Stubbs_c.1765_Turf, with Jockey up, at Newmarket.jpg
artwork_title: Turf, with Jockey up, at Newmarket
artwork_description: **George Stubbs** painted this intimate racing portrait around 1765, showing the racehorse *Turf* with a jockey mounted and ready for action at *Newmarket*, the headquarters of English thoroughbred racing. The horse is presented in the characteristic three-quarter view that Stubbs perfected for individual racehorse portraits, the jockey's silks and cap recorded with documentary precision. *Newmarket Heath* provided the backdrop for many of Stubbs's finest equine commissions in the 1760s, as aristocratic patrons competed to have their prized thoroughbreds immortalized by the country's most accomplished animal painter. Stubbs's anatomical training ensured that every muscle and joint of the horse was correctly placed, lending the image a truthfulness unmatched by any rival. ***Now at the *Yale Center for British Art*, it is a refined and authoritative example of the English racehorse portrait at its most accomplished.***

artwork_file: George Stubbs_1800_Freeman, the Earl of Clarendon's Gamekeeper, with a Dying Doe and Hound.jpg
artwork_title: Freeman, the Earl of Clarendon's Gamekeeper, with a Dying Doe and Hound
artwork_description: **George Stubbs** painted this sombre late work in 1800, depicting Freeman, the gamekeeper to the Earl of Clarendon, standing over a dying doe with a hound at his side. The composition has an elegiac quality unusual in sporting painting, the doe's suffering rendered without sentimentality but with a gravity that transforms a routine hunting scene into something approaching a meditation on mortality. Stubbs's late style, seen here at its most concentrated, strips the image to its essentials: three figures - man, dog, and prey - united in a final ritual of the chase. Freeman is painted as a fully realised individual, demonstrating Stubbs's lifelong insistence on treating working figures with the same dignity as his aristocratic patrons. ***Now at the *Yale Center for British Art*, it is one of the most powerful and affecting paintings of Stubbs's old age.***

artwork_file: George Stubbs_1761-1762_Molly Long-legs with her Jockey.jpg
artwork_description: **George Stubbs** painted this early equestrian portrait in 1761-1762, immortalizing the celebrated racehorse *Molly Long-legs* with her jockey up and ready for the course. Molly Long-legs was a real thoroughbred mare, and Stubbs's portrait captures her with the alert, high-strung energy characteristic of a finely prepared racehorse; the descriptive nickname alludes to her elegant, long-legged build. The painting dates from the very beginning of Stubbs's London career, when aristocratic commissions for racehorse portraits were transforming his reputation and finances. Now in the collection of *National Museums Liverpool*, the work connects Stubbs's first years of success to his hometown's cultural heritage. ***It is an early and accomplished example of the individual racehorse portrait, a genre Stubbs would perfect over the following decades.***

artwork_file: George Stubbs_1785_Reapers.jpg
artwork_description: **George Stubbs** painted *Reapers* in 1785 as one half of a celebrated pair of pastoral compositions, the other being *Haymakers*, both now at *Tate Britain*. The painting shows agricultural labourers - men and women together - engaged in the harvest, their figures arranged with the formal dignity of a classical frieze against a wide English landscape. Stubbs brings to labouring country people the same attentive particularity he applied to horses and their grooms, refusing to idealise them into mere pastoral decoration while maintaining a stately compositional harmony. The *Reapers* and *Haymakers* pair represents Stubbs's most ambitious engagement with rural genre painting, drawing on the pastoral tradition while grounding it in honest observation. ***Together they stand as a unique achievement in 18th-century British art, bridging country sport, natural history, and social portraiture.***

artwork_file: George Stubbs_c.1760_Foxhound on the Scent.jpg
artwork_medium: Oil on paper laid on board
artwork_description: **George Stubbs** painted this dynamic study of a foxhound in full hunting mode around 1760, the dog's body lowered and nose to the ground as it follows an invisible trail across open country. Executed in oil on paper laid on board, the work has an immediacy and freshness that suggests rapid observation from life, the hound's straining posture captured with great exactitude. Stubbs's knowledge of canine anatomy was as thorough as his mastery of the horse; he treated every species he depicted as a scientific subject worthy of careful study. The painting belongs to a group of closely related hound studies produced around this time and functions both as an autonomous work and as a study for larger compositions. ***It is a vivid and precise record of a working hunting dog, charged with the urgency of the chase.***

artwork_file: George Stubbs_1786_Bulls Fighting.jpg
artwork_description: **George Stubbs** painted this powerful and unusual work in 1786, depicting two bulls locked in violent combat in an open landscape, their massive bodies straining against each other in a contest of raw strength. The subject has an evident kinship with Stubbs's celebrated *Lion and Horse* series, channelling a similar interest in animal aggression and the drama of natural conflict that anticipates the Romantic movement. The painting displays Stubbs's characteristic ability to combine precise anatomical observation with genuine dramatic force, the bulls rendered as formidable natural presences rather than mere farm animals. The composition is boldly asymmetrical, the two animals filling the picture space with a concentrated energy that leaves little room for landscape. ***Now at the *Yale Center for British Art*, it is one of the most viscerally compelling animal paintings of the 18th century.***
